author: @imagingresourcereviews
text: Any update on availability? I'd love to get my hands on some of this stuff, wonder if it would work against hardened-on soap film on shower doors? We have a rental property in an area with hard water, and the shower doors weren't cleaned properly for years. So now there's a nearly-impervious soap scum film (wax and water minerals) on them that I'm having a really hard time getting off. Dawn dishwashing liquid mixed with vinegar has been somewhat effective, but there's more left. I wonder if Ecofoam could handle it? (I'm in the US, by the way, but would pay for the shipping here if you only  have distribution in the UK...)

text: the thing about soap is it is me not to work well I believe this. It is mean to be weak and inefficient so you use more and more of it in the company can make more and more money. I make my own lye soap in my life so Works 10 times faster literally than anything on the market. I never even come up with a lye soap that is absolutely unbelievable it has a couple of secret ingredients in it all natural and it strips grease off of your hands like nobody's business leaving the pink and perfect in seconds. I doubt very much that I could get a patent on it because it's probably been done already. I just use it for the sake of using something that works well and I give it to family and friends as well. author: @joinedupjon
text:
Someone's mentioned commercial wall mounted soap dispensers already - that's a market you might want to look at. My sister has a small business and uses foam type soap dispensers in the customer toilets. People use less liquid soap from foam type dispensers because (I guess) a small amount of soap feels like a good handful when foamed and it wets the skin very readily. She's paying over a tenner for a 1L container of soap. The commercial soap dispenser business seems to operate on the same principle as inkjet printers - the dispenser box is remarkably cheap and then they get you on the refills.
I guess you might look at ways of making a refill kit for the existing manufacturers systems or doing your own wall box.

all the best

replies.author: @lisakingscott7729
replies.text:
Well if it was easy, the billions being spent by EU, Tesla, etc. would have produced batteries with 100kWh/kg or more by now ;)
I think it's admirable that even if Rob produces nothing (and I think he has many things up his sleeve which will come to fruition!!!), at least he's inspiring others to play in this field. When you start investigating it, the almost infinite variety of chemicals and chemical structures that can be combined to make batteries and capacitors alone would take decades or even centuries to investigate all of them fully by one person.
There's nothing I've seen here which is pseudo science. It's all real science, which you can verify elsewhere on the web in scientific papers etc. Just google 'free energy' for the real pseudo science cr**.
Why not take up Rob's challenge and try it for yourself, anyone can do it, and maybe you would not be so critical.

replies.author: @matthiasBdot
replies.text:
No one expects it to be easy. At least I want Robert to find a competing product even if it's not higher capacities than current tech, it could have other benefits. Mabe a cheap production line independent of lithium. Please succeed!
What I wanted to express is: I'd rather like to see repeatedly videos of failing attempts producing such a battery and exploring its features instead of opinion talks or soap commercials showing up in my subscription feed.
Robert you might check your YouTube Analytics. Retention and Subscriptions might be interesting. There you can see video-vise that I'm probably not the only one disliking such content. At least I let you know. ...but maybe I'm simply wrong
